Kaplan and Norton (1992)

**Key Insight:**
What you measure is what you get - measure strategically important outcomes AND their drivers

sections.title: Four Perspectives
rows: How do we look to shareholders?
Can we improve and create value?

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Cause and Effect
sections.content:
**BSC Logic Flow (bottom to top):**

**Learning & Growth →**
Trained employees, good systems
↓
**Internal Process →**
Efficient operations, quality products
↓
**Customer →**
Satisfied customers, market share
↓
**Financial**
Revenue, profit, shareholder value

**Remember:**
Learning & Growth is the FOUNDATION
Financial results are the OUTCOME

**Strategy Map:**
Visualizes these cause-effect relationships

sections.title: Perspective Deep Dive
sections.content:
**Financial Perspective:**
• Lagging indicators - results
• Revenue growth, cost reduction, asset utilization
• Different by strategy stage

**Customer Perspective:**
• Market share, customer acquisition
• Customer retention, satisfaction
• Customer profitability

**Internal Process:**
• Innovation process (new products)
• Operations process (quality, time, cost)
• Post-sale service

**Learning & Growth:**
• Employee capabilities (training, skills)
• Information systems
• Motivation and alignment

sections.title: Leading vs Lagging Indicators
sections.content:
**Lagging Indicators:**
• Measure outcomes/results
• Historical
• Financial measures typically lagging
• Examples: Profit, market share

**Leading Indicators:**
• Predict future performance
• Actionable
• Non-financial often leading
• Examples: Training hours, customer satisfaction

**Balance Both:**
• Lagging shows if strategy worked
• Leading shows if you're on track
• Each objective should have both

**Key:**
'Lead' indicators today drive 'lagging' results tomorrow

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: BSC Problems
sections.content:
**Common BSC Implementation Issues:**

**Too Many Measures:**
• Aim for 15-25 total, not 100
• Focus on vital few

**Weak Linkages:**
• Must have cause-effect logic
• Not just a list of metrics

**Only Financial Focus:**
• Defeats the purpose
• Must balance perspectives

**No Strategy Link:**
• Measures must derive from strategy
• Not just 'good to know' metrics

**Set and Forget:**
• Review and adjust measures
• Cascaded to individuals

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: BSC: Four perspectives - Financial, Customer, Internal, Learning
Purpose: Translate strategy into measurable objectives
Cause-effect: Learning/Growth → Process → Customer → Financial
Strategy map: Visualizes linkages between objectives
Leading indicators: Predict; Lagging indicators: Result
Balance both: Non-financial lead to financial outcomes
Limit to 15-25 measures; link to strategy

knowledgeCheck.question: Employee training hours and employee satisfaction would be classified under which Balanced Scorecard perspective?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Learning and Growth focuses on employee capabilities, information systems, and organizational culture. Training hours and employee satisfaction are key measures of the organization's ability to improve and innovate, which support the other three perspectives.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which perspective focuses on employee capabilities and development?

title: Responsibility Centers and Transfer Pricing
description: Structure accountability and price internal transactions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Organizations need structure for accountability! CMAs design responsibility accounting systems and transfer pricing policies. These decisions determine how managers are evaluated and can significantly impact decision-making behavior. This is a heavily tested CMA topic.

sections.title: Responsibility Center Types
sections.content:
**Cost Center:**
• Manager controls costs only
• No revenue responsibility
• Example: Manufacturing, HR
• Performance: Cost variance, efficiency

**Revenue Center:**
• Manager controls revenue
• Limited cost control
• Example: Sales department
• Performance: Revenue vs budget

**Profit Center:**
• Manager controls revenue AND costs
• Example: Business unit
• Performance: Profit margin

**Investment Center:**
• All of profit center PLUS asset decisions
• Example: Division, subsidiary
• Performance: ROI, EVA, RI

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Transfer Pricing
sections.content:
**Why Transfer Pricing Matters:**

When Division A sells to Division B:
• Higher transfer price = Higher profit for A, lower for B
• Affects manager evaluation!
• Should not affect company-wide profit

**Three Approaches:**

**1. Market-Based:**
• Use external market price
• Best when market exists
• Fair benchmark

**2. Cost-Based:**
• Variable cost, full cost, cost-plus
• When no external market
• Easy to calculate

**3. Negotiated:**
• Divisions agree on price
• Reflects bargaining power
• May be contentious

sections.title: Transfer Pricing Range
sections.content:
**General Range:**

**Minimum (Selling Division):**
= Variable cost + Opportunity cost

**Maximum (Buying Division):**
= Lower of:
  - Market price
  - Contribution from buying outside

**Example:**
• Variable cost: $30
• Selling division at full capacity
• Market price for product: $50
• Contribution margin if sold externally: $20

**Minimum:** $30 + $20 = $50 (must cover opportunity cost)
**Maximum:** $50 (market price)

**Range is $50 - transfer at market!

sections.title: Transfer Pricing Issues
sections.content:
**Goal Congruence:**
• Division decisions should benefit company
• Wrong price = sub-optimal decisions

**Autonomy:**
• Divisions have freedom to negotiate
• Headquarters intervention undermines

**Performance Evaluation:**
• Both divisions should be measured fairly
• Transfer price affects profit

**Capacity Issues:**
• Excess capacity: Variable cost OK
• Full capacity: Must cover opportunity cost

**International:**
• Tax implications
• Arm's length requirement

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Make or Buy with Idle Capacity
sections.content:
**Transfer price depends on capacity!**

**Selling Division has EXCESS Capacity:**
• Opportunity cost = $0
• Minimum TP = Variable cost
• Should transfer internally

**Selling Division at FULL Capacity:**
• Opportunity cost = CM from external sales
• Minimum TP = Variable cost + Foregone CM
• May be better to buy externally

**Common Exam Mistake:**
Ignoring opportunity cost when at capacity

**Decision Rule:**
For the COMPANY - compare total relevant costs
Internal: Variable cost
External: Purchase price

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Centers: Cost (costs), Revenue, Profit (revenue + costs), Investment (+ assets)
Match measures to manager's control sphere
Transfer pricing: Market, cost-based, negotiated
Minimum TP: Variable cost + Opportunity cost
Maximum TP: Market price (or value to buying division)
With excess capacity: Transfer at variable cost OK
At full capacity: Include opportunity cost in minimum

knowledgeCheck.question: Division A makes a component with $25 variable cost. It's at full capacity and can sell externally for $40. What is the minimum transfer price to Division B?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Minimum transfer price = Variable cost + Opportunity cost. At full capacity, selling internally means giving up $40 - $25 = $15 contribution margin. Minimum = $25 + $15 = $40. Division A needs the transfer to be as beneficial as external sales.
knowledgeCheck.hint: At full capacity, include the foregone contribution margin.

title: Customer and Product Profitability Analysis
description: Analyze profitability by customer and product

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Not all customers and products are equally profitable! CMAs provide insights that drive strategic decisions about where to focus resources. Understanding profitability at the customer and product level enables better pricing, resource allocation, and strategic planning.

sections.title: Profitability Analysis Overview
sections.content:
**Why Analyze Profitability?**
• Identify profitable vs unprofitable segments
• Support pricing decisions
• Allocate resources effectively
• Improve customer/product mix
• Guide strategic investments

**Key Principle:**
• Revenue doesn't equal profitability
• High-revenue customers may be unprofitable
• Small customers may be highly profitable

**Pareto Principle (80/20):**
• Often 20% of customers = 80% of profits
• Some customers actually destroy value

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Customer Profitability Analysis
sections.content:
**CPA Formula:**

Customer Profitability =
Revenue from customer
- Direct product costs
- Customer-specific costs
- Allocated support costs

**Customer-Specific Costs:**
• Order processing (# orders × cost/order)
• Delivery ($$ per delivery)
• Special handling/packaging
• Returns processing
• Customer service time
• Discounts and allowances

**Use ABC to trace costs!**

**Whale Curve:**
Graph showing cumulative profit by customer
Most profitable to least
Shape shows where value created/destroyed

sections.title: Product Profitability Analysis
sections.content:
**Product Profitability:**
Revenue - All costs traceable to product

**Direct Costs:**
• Materials, labor, variable manufacturing

**Traceable Indirect:**
• Equipment dedicated to product
• Product-specific marketing
• Product manager salary
• Product-line warehousing

**Challenges:**
• Common costs allocation
• Joint product situations
• Related product effects

**Contribution Hierarchy:**
• Unit-level
• Batch-level
• Product-level
• Facility-level

sections.title: Customer Lifetime Value
sections.content:
**CLV Definition:**
PV of all future profits from a customer

**CLV Formula (Simple):**
CLV = (Avg margin × Retention rate) / (1 + Discount rate - Retention rate)

**Factors:**
• Average purchase value
• Purchase frequency
• Customer lifespan (retention)
• Acquisition cost
• Service costs

**Strategic Use:**
• How much to spend on acquisition?
• Which customers to retain?
• Where to invest in relationships?
• Prioritize high-CLV customers

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Dropping Products/Customers
sections.content:
**Don't Drop Unless:**
• Fixed costs will actually decrease
• Or capacity can be used better

**Contribution Margin Rule:**
• Positive CM products contribute to fixed costs
• Dropping them may not improve profits
• Unless AVOIDABLE fixed costs > CM

**Customer Considerations:**
• Unprofitable customers might become profitable
• Reference value (attract other customers)
• Growth potential
• Service cost reduction opportunities

**Better Options:**
• Raise prices
• Reduce service levels
• Change terms (min order size)
• Charge for services

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Revenue ≠ Profitability - analyze at customer/product level
Customer profitability = Revenue - Direct - Customer-specific costs
Use ABC to trace customer/product costs accurately
Pareto: Often 20% of customers = 80% of profits
CLV: Present value of future customer profits
Don't drop products with positive contribution margin
Consider alternatives: repricing, service changes, minimums

knowledgeCheck.question: Customer A generates $500K revenue with $350K product costs and $180K customer-specific costs. Customer A is:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Customer profitability = Revenue - Product costs - Customer-specific costs = $500K - $350K - $180K = -$30K. Customer A is unprofitable by $30,000. This customer destroys value despite generating significant revenue.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Subtract all traceable costs from revenue.

title: Economic Value Added (EVA)
description: Measure true economic profit creation

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Accounting profit doesn't capture true value creation! EVA measures whether a company earns more than its cost of capital. CMAs use EVA to evaluate business unit performance, guide investment decisions, and align management incentives with shareholder value.

sections.title: EVA Concept
sections.content:
**The Problem with Accounting Profit:**
• Doesn't consider cost of equity capital
• Debt interest is expensed, but not equity cost
• Can show profit while destroying value

**Economic Value Added (EVA):**
• Profit after ALL capital costs
• Equals residual income concept
• Developed by Stern Stewart & Co.
• Also called Economic Profit

**Key Insight:**
Positive EVA = Value created
Negative EVA = Value destroyed

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: EVA Calculation
sections.content:
**Basic EVA Formula:**

**EVA = NOPAT - (Invested Capital × WACC)**

**Example:**
• EBIT: $1,000,000
• Tax rate: 30%
• NOPAT: $700,000
• Invested capital: $5,000,000
• WACC: 10%
• Capital charge: $500,000

**EVA = $700,000 - $500,000 = $200,000**

**Interpretation:**
Company earned $200,000 ABOVE its cost of capital
True economic profit = $200,000
Value created for shareholders

**Alternative View:**
ROIC = $700,000 / $5,000,000 = 14%
Spread = 14% - 10% = 4% × $5,000,000 = $200,000

sections.title: EVA vs ROI
sections.content:
**Return on Investment (ROI):**
• Percentage measure
• May reject value-creating projects
• Doesn't consider size
• Can manipulate by avoiding investments

**EVA Advantages:**
• Dollar measure (size matters)
• Encourages value-creating investments
• Managers won't reject good projects
• Aligns with shareholder value

**Example - ROI Problem:**
Division with 25% ROI rejects 18% project
But if WACC = 10%, project creates value!
ROI goes down, but EVA goes up
→ EVA gives correct signal

sections.title: NOPAT Adjustments
sections.content:
**Common Adjustments to Get NOPAT:**

**Add Back:**
• Interest expense (tax-effected)
• R&D capitalization (if expensed)
• Operating lease interest (old GAAP)
• Goodwill amortization (if applicable)

**Rationale:**
• Remove financing effects
• Capitalize investments that create future value
• Compare operations, not capital structure

**Invested Capital:**
• Total debt + Equity
• Or: Total assets - Non-interest-bearing liabilities
• Represents capital providers' investment

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: EVA and Investment Decisions
sections.content:
**EVA vs ROI - Critical Difference:**

**Scenario:**
Division ROI = 20%, WACC = 12%
New project: ROI = 15%, requires $1M capital

**Using ROI:**
Manager rejects (15% < current 20%)
Division ROI stays at 20%
✘ WRONG decision!

**Using EVA:**
Project EVA = 15% - 12% = 3% × $1M = $30,000
Positive EVA = Accept!
✔ CORRECT decision!

**Key Point:**
EVA accepts any project earning above WACC
ROI may reject value-creating projects

**CMA Exam:**
Understand why EVA aligns incentives better than ROI

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: EVA = NOPAT - (Invested Capital × WACC)
Measures profit AFTER all capital costs
Positive EVA = Value created for shareholders
Negative EVA = Value destroyed
Better than ROI for investment decisions
ROI may reject good projects; EVA won't
NOPAT = After-tax operating profit

knowledgeCheck.question: A division has NOPAT of $400,000, invested capital of $2,500,000, and WACC is 12%. What is EVA?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: EVA = NOPAT - Capital charge = $400,000 - ($2,500,000 × 12%) = $400,000 - $300,000 = $100,000. This division earned $100,000 above its cost of capital.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the capital charge first (Capital × WACC).

title: Balanced Scorecard Implementation
description: Link strategy to operational metrics across perspectives

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Financial metrics alone don't tell the whole story! The Balanced Scorecard links strategy to operational measures across four perspectives. CMAs often lead BSC implementation to drive organizational performance.

sections.title: BSC Overview
sections.content:
**Balanced Scorecard (BSC):**
• Strategic performance management system
• Developed by Kaplan and Norton
• Balances financial and non-financial measures
• Links metrics to strategy

**Why 'Balanced'?**
• Financial AND operational
• Leading AND lagging indicators
• Internal AND external focus
• Short-term AND long-term

**Purpose:**
• Translate strategy into measurable objectives
• Communicate strategy organization-wide
• Align activities with strategic goals

sections.title: Four Perspectives
rows: How do we look to shareholders?

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Perspective Hierarchy
sections.content:
**Cause-and-Effect Logic:**

**Learning & Growth** (Foundation)
→ Skilled, motivated employees

**Internal Processes** (Operational)
→ Better operations and innovation

**Customer** (External)
→ Satisfied, loyal customers

**Financial** (Results)
→ Strong financial performance

**Memory:**
'Learn to process, satisfy customers, make money'

**Key Insight:**
Leading indicators (L&G, Process) drive
Lagging indicators (Customer, Financial)

sections.title: Building a Strategy Map
sections.content:
**Strategy Map:**
• Visual representation of strategy
• Shows cause-and-effect relationships
• Connects objectives across perspectives

**Components:**
• Strategic objectives within each perspective
• Cause-effect arrows between objectives
• Strategic themes grouping related objectives

**Example Chain:**
Train employees (L&G)
→ Reduce process errors (Internal)
→ Improve quality scores (Customer)
→ Increase profit margins (Financial)

**Benefits:**
• Clarifies strategy
• Shows how activities drive results
• Communication tool

sections.title: KPIs and Targets
sections.content:
**For Each Objective, Define:**
• **Measure (KPI):** How to track it
• **Target:** What level to achieve
• **Initiative:** Actions to reach target

**Good KPI Criteria:**
• Relevant to objective
• Measurable quantitatively
• Controllable by responsible party
• Timely data available

**Leading vs Lagging:**
• **Leading:** Predicts future (training hours)
• **Lagging:** Confirms results (revenue)
• Need both for balanced view

**Typical BSC:** 15-25 measures total
Avoid too many - focus on key drivers

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: BSC Mistakes
sections.content:
**Common Implementation Failures:**

**Too Many Measures:**
• Trying to measure everything
• Focus on 4-7 per perspective

**No Cause-Effect Links:**
• Random metrics, not strategic
• Must link to strategy

**Set and Forget:**
• Must review and update regularly
• Strategy evolves

**Only Financial Focus:**
• Defeats purpose of BSC
• All four perspectives matter

**Exam Tip:**
Know which measures go with which perspective
L&G = Employee/capability measures
Process = Operational efficiency
Customer = External satisfaction
Financial = Money metrics

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: BSC: Four perspectives - Financial, Customer, Process, Learning
Links strategy to measurable objectives
Balances financial with non-financial measures
Strategy map shows cause-and-effect relationships
Learning/Process drive Customer/Financial results
Each objective needs measure, target, initiative
Leading indicators predict; lagging confirm results

knowledgeCheck.question: Employee training hours per year would be a measure in which Balanced Scorecard perspective?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Learning & Growth perspective focuses on organizational capacity to improve, including employee skills, training, and development. Employee training hours is a measure of investment in human capital, which is the foundation of the BSC.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which perspective focuses on employee development and capability?

title: Key Performance Indicators and Dashboards
description: Select and visualize effective performance metrics

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: What gets measured gets managed! CMAs design KPI systems and dashboards that drive organizational performance. Selecting the right metrics and presenting them effectively is a critical management accounting skill.

sections.title: KPI Fundamentals
sections.content:
**Key Performance Indicator (KPI):**
A quantifiable measure used to evaluate success in meeting objectives

**Characteristics of Good KPIs:**
• **Specific:** Clearly defined
• **Measurable:** Quantifiable
• **Achievable:** Realistic targets
• **Relevant:** Aligned to strategy
• **Time-bound:** Defined period

**KPI Hierarchy:**
• Strategic KPIs (board level)
• Tactical KPIs (management level)
• Operational KPIs (department level)
• Cascade and align throughout organization

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: KPI Selection
sections.content:
**Selection Criteria:**

**Strategic Alignment:**
• Does it support strategic objectives?
• Does leadership care about it?

**Actionability:**
• Can we influence the result?
• Will it drive behavior?

**Data Availability:**
• Can we measure it accurately?
• Is data timely enough?

**Balance:**
• Mix of leading and lagging
• Financial and non-financial
• Short and long-term

**Less is More:**
• 5-7 KPIs per area
• Too many = no focus
• 'Dashboard of everything' fails

sections.title: Dashboard Design
sections.content:
**Dashboard Purpose:**
• Visual display of key metrics
• At-a-glance performance view
• Enables quick decisions

**Design Principles:**
• **Simplicity:** Not cluttered
• **Focus:** Most important metrics first
• **Context:** Show trends, comparisons
• **Actionable:** Drive decisions
• **Accurate:** Real-time or near-real-time

**Visual Elements:**
• Gauges for single metrics vs target
• Line charts for trends
• Bar charts for comparisons
• Tables for detailed data
• Color coding (red/yellow/green)

sections.title: Common Dashboard Mistakes
sections.content:
**Mistakes to Avoid:**

**Too Many Metrics:**
• Information overload
• No clear priorities
• Decision paralysis

**Wrong Visualizations:**
• Pie charts for many categories
• 3D charts that distort
• Inconsistent scales

**Lack of Context:**
• Numbers without targets
• No historical comparison
• Missing variance explanation

**Poor Updating:**
• Stale data
• Manual processes
• Inconsistent refresh

**Not Audience-Appropriate:**
• Executive dashboard ≠ operational
• Match detail to user needs

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: KPI vs Metric
sections.content:
**All KPIs are Metrics, but Not All Metrics are KPIs**

**Metric:**
• Any quantifiable measure
• May or may not be important
• Hundreds possible in organization
• Example: # of emails sent

**KPI:**
• KEY metrics - most important
• Directly tied to strategic objectives
• Limited set (5-20 total)
• Example: Customer acquisition cost

**Upgrade Path:**
• Metric becomes KPI when:
  - Linked to strategy
  - Has target
  - Has accountability
  - Drives action

**Exam Tip:**
Look for strategic relevance when identifying KPIs

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: KPIs: Key metrics tied to strategic objectives
SMART: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
Balance leading/lagging, financial/non-financial
Less is more - 5-7 KPIs per area
Dashboards: Visual display for at-a-glance decisions
Show context: trends, targets, comparisons
Match dashboard detail level to audience

knowledgeCheck.question: A company tracks 'number of customer complaints in queue' as a performance metric. This is best classified as a:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Customer complaints in queue is a LEADING indicator (predicts potential future churn/satisfaction) and OPERATIONAL (non-financial, process-based). It predicts future outcomes rather than confirming past results.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Does this metric predict future results or confirm past performance? Is it money or operations?
